title: Making vermicompost tea from wormery drainage
subtitle: Collecting and using the liquid plant feed produced by a wormery
excerpt: A wormery produces two outputs: solid vermicompost and a liquid known as worm leachate or vermicompost tea. The liquid drains into the base tray and should be collected every 1-2 weeks. Diluted 1:10 with water, it makes a liquid plant feed rich in humic acids and soluble nutrients. Undiluted, it can burn plant roots.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic worm composting guidance on leachate use. RHS liquid feeding guidance for vegetables and container plants. Rothamsted Research data on humic acid content of vermicompost leachate. Mary Appelhof Worms Eat My Garbage (1982) on vermicompost liquid feeding.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dark liquid that drains from a wormery base tray. A by-product of worm composting, rich in humic acids, soluble nutrients, and beneficial microorganisms when diluted.
A contained composting system using red wiggler worms (Eisenia fetida) to convert kitchen waste into compost and liquid plant feed.

content.text: The liquid that collects in the base tray of a

. A well-fed wormery processing 2-3 kg of scraps per week produces around 100-200 ml of leachate per day in warm weather. The liquid is dark brown and smells earthy. It contains humic acids, fulvic acids, soluble nitrogen, potassium, phosphorus, and beneficial microorganisms from the worm gut. Undiluted it is too concentrated for direct plant use; diluted 1:10 it is a useful supplement to regular watering.

Most three-tray wormery systems have a tap or drain plug on the base tray. Collect the leachate every 1-2 weeks by placing a bucket under the tap and opening it briefly. If the wormery is indoors, a small bowl will do. Do not let leachate build up in the base tray to the point where the lowest working tray sits in liquid: this drowns worms.
General vegetable watering: dilute 1:10 (100 ml leachate per litre of water). Apply to the soil around plants, not directly on leaves. Use weekly during the growing season as a supplement to feeding.
Seedlings: dilute more heavily, 1:20, as seedlings are sensitive to salt and concentrated nutrients.
Containers and hanging baskets: 1:10, fortnightly. Container plants benefit from the microorganism content as well as the nutrients.

Drain treatment: undiluted or 1:5, poured down kitchen and bathroom drains. The beneficial bacteria help break down organic matter and reduce blockage risk. Not a replacement for clearing a blocked drain.

Worm leachate is sold as worm tea, described as a complete plant fertiliser. The nutrient content varies with what the worms have eaten. Treat it as a useful supplement: approximately equivalent to a dilute seaweed or comfrey feed: rather than as a replacement for potash-rich feeds for fruiting crops or high-nitrogen feeds for leafy vegetables.
